1: A theme: the psalmist's cry to "my Rock" establishes the pattern of the righteous sufferer calling on YHWH in silence — and the cry that will not be answered by silence is the cry that grounds the NT's confidence in prayer. 1 Corinthians 10:4 identifies the rock that accompanied Israel as Christ himself; Psalm 28's "my Rock, do not be deaf to me" is the prayer directed toward the one who is both the foundation of all prayer and its ultimate answer. The terror of divine silence — "if you remain silent, I will be like those who go down to the pit" — captures the existential stakes of the cross, when the one who prayed "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" experienced the anguished version of this fear.
2: A theme: lifting hands toward the Most Holy Place (devir) — the innermost sanctuary housing the ark-throne — is the prayer posture that the entire Levitical system made available to Israel. Christ fulfills this gesture as the great high priest who entered the true Most Holy Place by means of his own blood (Hebrews 9:11-12, 24-26), making effective the approach that the lifted hands of the psalmist could only symbolize. The prayer that rises toward the earthly devir now rises through the heavenly high priest who stands permanently in the presence of God on behalf of those who call to him (Hebrews 7:25).
3: A theme: "speak peace to their neighbors while plotting evil in their hearts" — the psalm's description of the wicked who use words of peace to conceal hearts of violence identifies precisely the kind of duplicity that Christ repudiated throughout his ministry (Matthew 15:8: "this people honors me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me"). Christ himself is the inverse: his words of peace are expressions of a heart that is entirely for the good of those he addresses. The distinction between the righteous sufferer (the psalmist, and ultimately Christ) and those who speak peace while plotting evil is the moral contrast that the entire passion narrative makes visible.
4: A theme: the imprecation against the wicked — "pay them back for what they have done" — stands within the covenant framework where YHWH is the judge who repays. The NT does not repudiate divine judgment but relocates and extends its scope: the wrath that the wicked deserve has fallen on Christ at the cross (Romans 3:25-26; 2 Corinthians 5:21), and the judgment that remains is the final judgment of the last day. The psalmist's prayer for just repayment is not vindictiveness but covenant realism: the moral order of the universe requires that wrong be answered with consequence. Christ's atonement is the answer to the same moral logic — not its elimination but its satisfaction at infinite cost.
5: A theme: "they take no account of the LORD's works" — the blindness to divine activity that results in destruction. The pattern runs throughout the OT (Isaiah 5:12: "they do not regard the deeds of the LORD, or see the work of his hands") and is fulfilled in those who failed to recognize Christ's signs and wonders as divine works (John 12:37: "though he had performed so many signs in their presence, they still would not believe in him"). To take no account of divine works is ultimately to miss the greatest divine work — the incarnation, death, and resurrection of the Son.
6: A theme: "Praise the LORD! For he has heard my cry for mercy" — the pivot from lament to praise that structures the individual lament genre and the entire gospel narrative. The cry that is heard is the pattern of the cross-and-resurrection: the crucified one's cry (Psalms 22:24) is answered by the resurrection. Hebrews 5:7 applies this directly to Christ: "In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence." The same YHWH who heard the psalmist heard the incarnate Son; the pivot from lament to praise is the paschal movement.
7: A theme: "The LORD is my strength and my shield; my heart trusts in him and I am helped; my heart leaps for joy, and with my song I give thanks to him." The experience of help received that produces trust confirmed and joy expressed is the movement of faith through suffering to vindication. Ephesians 6:16 identifies "the shield of faith" as armor against the enemy's attacks; Psalms 28:7's "shield" is the covenant protection that faith grasps as already present. The sequence — strength, shield, trust, help, joy, thanks — maps the spiritual journey from vulnerability through faith to doxology that the NT presents as the normal pattern of Christian suffering (Romans 5:3-5; 2 Corinthians 4:17).
8: A direct anticipation: "The LORD is the strength of his people; he is the saving fortress of his anointed one (meshiho)." The Messianic title appears explicitly: YHWH is the strength of and salvation for his mashiach (anointed one). In the royal psalms, the anointed one is the Davidic king whom YHWH supports; in the NT's canonical reading, this support-relationship between YHWH and his anointed is the pattern of the Father's support of the Son. The resurrection is the supreme instance of YHWH being the "saving fortress" of his anointed: the one who died is raised and vindicated by the Father (Acts 2:36; Philippians 2:9-11).
9: A theme: "Save your people and bless your heritage; be their shepherd and carry them forever." The shepherd who carries his people — the divine shepherd of Israel — is fulfilled in Christ, who calls himself the Good Shepherd (John 10:11), who carries the lost sheep on his shoulders (Luke 15:5), and who is identified as the "great Shepherd of the sheep" by the blood of the eternal covenant (Hebrews 13:20). "Carry them forever" anticipates the eternal shepherding of Revelation 7:17: "the Lamb at the center of the throne will be their shepherd; he will lead them to springs of living water." The prayer of v9 receives its complete answer only in the eschatological shepherd-community of the new creation.
